source_short_name: Rectangular grid lasers; parity forces a walk; count configs mod 1000
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1
schema_version: 1
identity:
  family_id: directed_laser_edges_on_rect_grid_junctions__blocked_moves_between_cells__parity_chain_obstruction__count_all_configs_mod
  instance_id: W7_H5_interior24__beautiful_walk_always_exists__K_equals_4pow24_mod1000_2026_02_16
  short_name: Rectangular grid lasers; parity forces a walk; count configs mod 1000
  generation: 1
taxonomy:
  domain: combinatorics
  subdomain(s):
  - directed graphs on lattice grids
  - reachability in planar cell complexes
  - parity/handshaking invariants
  - configuration counting and modular reduction
  topic_path:
  - combinatorics
  - graph_theory
  - planar_graphs
  - reachability
  - parity_invariants
  - modular_counting
  tags:
  - rectangular_grid
  - directed_edges
  - blocked_adjacent_moves
  - reachable_region_boundary
  - handshaking_lemma
  - boundary_to_boundary_chain
  - always_reachable
  - count_configurations
  - CRT_mod_1000
problem_spec:
  summary: On a W×H rectangular grid of unit cells, each interior lattice junction emits exactly one directed unit laser segment to a neighboring junction. A move between adjacent cells is allowed iff their common side contains no laser. A Beautiful Walk is a path of adjacent cells from the southwest cell to the northeast cell using only allowed moves. For a specific rectangle (W,H), count configurations admitting at least one Beautiful Walk and reduce mod 1000.
  object(s):
  - name: W
    type: integer
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: H
    type: integer
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: junctions
    type: set
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: lasers
    type: configuration
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: grid_cells
    type: set
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: allowed_move
    type: predicate
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: walk
    type: sequence
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: K
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  - name: out
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - The grid is [0,W]×[0,H] with (W,H)=(7,5), tiled into W \cdot H unit square cabins.
  - Interior junctions are (a,b) with 1\leqa\leqW-1 and 1\leqb\leqH-1; each chooses exactly one directed unit segment to one of its four neighbors.
  - Boundary junctions emit no lasers. Laser segments may overlap.
  - A move between adjacent cabins is allowed iff their shared side contains no laser segment (either direction).
  - A Beautiful Walk starts at the southwest cabin and ends at the northeast cabin.
  - K is the number of configurations admitting at least one Beautiful Walk.
  ask:
  - Compute K mod 1000.
answer_spec:
  type: integer_mod_m
  normalization: mod_m
  modulus: 1000
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: width_W
    type: int
    domain: integer >= 2
    purpose: number of unit cabins horizontally (here 7)
  - name: height_H
    type: int
    domain: integer >= 2
    purpose: number of unit cabins vertically (here 5)
  - name: outdegree_rule
    type: constraint
    domain: each interior junction chooses exactly 1 of 4 directions
    purpose: defines configuration space size 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}
  - name: blocking_rule
    type: constraint
    domain: a shared side is blocked iff any laser segment lies along it
    purpose: induces allowed adjacency among cabins
  - name: endpoints
    type: pair
    domain: (southwest_cabin, northeast_cabin)
    purpose: start and finish cabins
  - name: modulus
    type: int
    domain: positive_integer
    purpose: final reduction (here 1000)
  invariant(s):
  - name: configuration_count
    depends_on:
    - width_W
    - height_H
    statement: There are (W-1)(H-1) interior junctions, each choosing one of 4 directions independently, so total configurations are 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}.
  - name: boundary_chain_parity_obstruction
    depends_on:
    - grid_cells
    statement: If the northeast cabin is not reachable from the southwest cabin using allowed moves, the interface between reachable and non-reachable cabins (shared sides) forms a set of grid segments whose interior junction degrees are even; thus it contains an open boundary-to-boundary chain.
  - name: directed_chain_impossibility
    depends_on:
    - outdegree_rule
    statement: 'A boundary-to-boundary chain of grid segments each containing a laser is impossible: boundary junctions have outdegree 0, and outdegree 1 at interior junctions forces all lasers along the chain to orient backward, eventually requiring a boundary junction to emit.'
  - name: always_reachable
    depends_on:
    - boundary_chain_parity_obstruction
    - directed_chain_impossibility
    statement: Since non-reachability would imply an impossible boundary-to-boundary laser chain, the northeast cabin is always reachable; therefore every configuration admits a Beautiful Walk.
  operator(s):
  - build_allowed_adjacency_graph_on_cells
  - define_reachable_region
  - construct_interface_segment_set
  - build_interface_graph
  - apply_handshaking_lemma
  - derive_boundary_to_boundary_path
  - propagate_direction_constraints_along_chain
  - modular_exponentiation
  - chinese_remainder_mod_1000
  essential_assumption(s):
  - Only interior junctions emit lasers; boundary junctions emit none.
  - Overlaps are allowed; any presence blocks a side.
  - Reachability is via 4-neighbor cabin adjacency only.
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - Prove every configuration admits a Beautiful Walk, so K equals the full configuration count 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}. Then K mod 1000 is uniquely determined by modular arithmetic (e.g., CRT).
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: count_total_configurations
    inputs:
    - width_W
    - height_H
    - outdegree_rule
    purpose: compute total configuration count as 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}
    outputs:
    - total_configurations
  - name: parity_force_boundary_to_boundary_chain
    inputs:
    - reachable_region
    - interface_segment_set
    purpose: if the target is unreachable, use parity/even-degree structure to force an open interface chain with boundary endpoints
    outputs:
    - boundary_to_boundary_chain
  - name: propagate_directions_contradiction
    inputs:
    - boundary_to_boundary_chain
    - outdegree_rule
    purpose: propagate forced laser orientations along the chain to contradict boundary outdegree 0
    outputs:
    - contradiction
  - name: conclude_all_configs_good
    inputs:
    - contradiction
    purpose: conclude the target is always reachable, so K equals total configurations
    outputs:
    - K_equals_total
  - name: compute_mod_1000_via_CRT
    inputs:
    - K_equals_total
    - modulus
    purpose: compute 4^{24} mod 1000 by combining mod 8 and mod 125 computations
    outputs:
    - out
  theorem(s):
  - name: handshaking_lemma
    role: a finite graph has an even number of odd-degree vertices; used to infer open path components
  - name: modular_exponentiation
    role: compute powers mod 125 efficiently
  - name: CRT_mod_1000
    role: combine congruences mod 8 and mod 125 into a unique mod 1000 residue
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Convert reachability failure to a planar interface, force a boundary-to-boundary chain by parity, then contradict the one-outgoing-laser rule, reducing counting to a pure exponentiation problem.
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Use the reachable/non-reachable interface and parity (even degrees at interior junctions) to force a boundary-to-boundary chain.
  - Show any such chain is impossible under the outdegree constraints and boundary outdegree 0 by direction propagation.
  - Therefore all configurations work, so K is the full count 4^{(W-1)(H-1)}, then reduce modulo 1000 via CRT.
  finish_type: invariant_contradiction_then_modular_count
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: builtins
    reason: Python's built-in pow(a,b,m) computes modular exponentiation exactly
  - name: itertools
    reason: optional brute-force enumeration for small (W,H) sanity checks
  import_hint(s):
  - '(none)  # pow is built-in; optional: from itertools import product'
  task(s):
  - name: verify_mod_result
    reason: compute pow(4,24,1000) and confirm 656
  - name: brute_force_small_rectangle_sanity
    reason: for small rectangles (e.g., 3×3), enumerate configurations and BFS reachability to confirm "always reachable"
well_posed: true
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    W: 7
    H: 5
    interior_junctions: 24
    total_configurations: 4^24
    modulus: 1000
  final_answer:
    value: 656
    normalized: 656

problem_spec:
  summary: Determine the guilty suspect in a four-person symbolic logic puzzle with exactly one all-lies speaker, exactly one guilty suspect, and statement formulas built from implication, Boolean connectives, and, in some instances, references to the truth values of other statements.
  object(s):
  - name: suspects
    type: set
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: L
    type: function
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: G
    type: function
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: P
    type: function
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: S
    type: function
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: culprit_index
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - 'At 22 : 05 the Amber Labyrinth Orb vanished from its locked pedestal.'
  - Four badge-holders (1 2 3 4) were logged nearby.
  - Exactly one suspect lies in every statement they utter; the other three speak only truths.
  - Exactly one suspect is guilty.
  - The universal liar and the thief are different people.
  - Suspect 1
  - (1) “If 2 is the universal liar, then 3 is definitely not the thief.”
  - '(2) “If 4 was at the pedestal at 22 : 05, then 2 cannot be the liar.”'
  - (3) “Statement 3-(2) is itself false.”
  - (4) “I am certainly not the thief.”
  - Suspect 2
  - (1) “I am the thief.”
  - '(2) “I was at the pedestal at 22 : 05.”'
  - (3) “Every line uttered by 3 is a lie.”
  - (4) “If 4 really was present, then 1 cannot be the liar.”
  - Suspect 3
  - (1) “Every word of 2 is false.”
  - '(2) “4 was nowhere near the pedestal at 22 : 05.”'
  - '(3) “Whether I saw 2 at 22 : 05 matches exactly whether 4 is the liar.”'
  - (4) “If 2 is the thief, then 4 is the liar.”
  - Suspect 4
  - (1) “I am not the thief.”
  - '(2) “I was indeed present at 22 : 05.”'
  - (3) “1 is the liar iff 3 is the thief.”
  - (4) “Should 1 turn out to be the liar, that forces 2 to be the thief.”
  ask:
  - Who stole the Amber Labyrinth Orb? Return a single digit 1–4.
answer_spec:
  type: integer
  normalization: none
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: statement_count_per_suspect
    type: other
    domain: list of four positive integers
    purpose: records the logical branching width for each suspect
  - name: liar_culprit_coupling
    type: enum
    domain:
      distinct: true
      unconstrained: false
    purpose: records whether the liar and culprit are forced to be different individuals
  invariant(s):
  - name: unique_liar
    depends_on:
    - L
    statement: Exactly one suspect lies in every statement; the remaining suspects are truthful in every statement.
  - name: unique_culprit
    depends_on:
    - G
    statement: Exactly one suspect is guilty.
  operator(s):
  - case_split_on_liar_identity
  - propositional_constraint_propagation
  - xor
  - implication
  - truth_value_propagation
  essential_assumption(s):
  - Each suspect is either wholly truthful or wholly dishonest across all of their statements.
  - The liar and the culprit are distinct individuals.
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - Finite case analysis over the possible liar identities leaves exactly one admissible culprit label.
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: world_consistency_elimination
    inputs:
    - candidate liar identity
    - candidate culprit identity
    - statement truth values
    purpose: discard assignments that violate the all-truth or all-lies rules
    outputs:
    - consistent worlds
  theorem(s):
  - name: classical_propositional_semantics
    role: determines the truth of every symbolic statement under a candidate world assignment
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - A finite logical world model can be checked exhaustively for consistency.
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Treat each candidate liar as a full world hypothesis and reject any hypothesis that makes one of the other suspects partially false.
  finish_type: finite_enumeration
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: itertools
    reason: enumerate candidate liar, culprit, and presence assignments exactly
  import_hint(s):
  - from itertools import product
  task(s):
  - name: symbolic_world_search
    reason: verify the unique culprit by exhaustive search over the finite truth assignment space
well_posed: 'yes'
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    statement_count_per_suspect:
    - 5
    - 4
    - 4
    - 4
    distinct_liar_and_culprit: true
  final_answer:
    value: '2'
    normalized: '2'

problem_spec:
  summary: Evaluate a Boolean formula on all satisfying valuations under an additional parity or threshold filter, encode each surviving valuation as an integer, and aggregate the encoded values by an explicit arithmetic expression modulo a fixed modulus.
  object(s):
  - name: valuation
    type: other
    role: unknown
    dependency: independent
  - name: formula
    type: function
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: encoding
    type: function
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: Sigma
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - For Boolean variables $p,q,r,s,t,u\in\{0,1\}$ write
  - • $p\to q=\lnot p\lor q$ (material implication)
  - • $p\oplus q=1$ iff $p\ne q$ (exclusive–or).
  - Define two predicates
  - $$
  - \begin{aligned}
  - G(p,q,r,s,t,u)\; &=\; \bigl[(p\oplus q)\oplus(r\to s)\bigr]\land\bigl[(s\oplus t)\to((\lnot p)\oplus r)\bigr],\\[4pt]
  - H(p,q,r,s,t,u)\; &=\; \bigl[(q\to r)\oplus(p\land u)\bigr]\land\bigl[(s\lor t)\oplus(\lnot r)\bigr].
  - \end{aligned}
  - $$
  ask:
  - Compute the stated modular aggregate over the surviving valuations.

  type: integer_mod_m
  normalization: mod_m

  - name: finite_search_space

  operator(s):
  - truth_table_evaluation

  - modular_reduction
  essential_assumption(s):

  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - Exhaustive evaluation of the finite truth table determines a single aggregate value before the final modular reduction.

    purpose: identify exactly which valuations contribute to the aggregate
    outputs:

    outputs:

source_short_name: Expected misread error under 4-hour window on a 12-hour clock with identical hands
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1
schema_version: 1
identity:
  family_id: indistinguishable_clock_hands__ambiguous_configurations__window_bounded__expected_error_fraction_sum
  instance_id: equal_length_hands_12hr__window4__expected_error__a_plus_b_2026_02_14
  short_name: Expected misread error under 4-hour window on a 12-hour clock with identical hands
  generation: 1
taxonomy:
  domain: discrete_math
  subdomain(s):
  - modular/time-cycle reasoning
  - fractional part constraints on real variables
  - discrete ambiguity classes from symmetry
  - finite counting and expected-value computation
  topic_path:
  - discrete_math
  - modular_arithmetic_analogs
  - functional_constraints
  - extremal_counting
  - expectation
  tags:
  - analog_clock
  - indistinguishable_hands
  - fractional_part
  - 12_hour_cycle
  - ambiguity_classes
  - integer_part_casework
  - expected_value
  - rational_output
problem_spec:
  summary: Ambiguous readings with indistinguishable clock hands are characterized by a fractional-part system that reduces to discrete integer-part parameters. Under a bounded-error window, count all admissible ambiguous configurations, compute the expected misread error, and output the sum of numerator and denominator of the reduced fraction.
  object(s):
  - name: h
    type: real
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: m
    type: real
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: i
    type: integer
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: j
    type: integer
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: E
    type: rational
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  - name: a
    type: integer
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: b
    type: integer
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: out
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - The clock is a 12-hour cycle and is perfect, but the hour and minute hands are indistinguishable.
  - A valid reading (h,m) with 0<=h,m<12 satisfies h = floor(h) + m/12.
  - A configuration {h,m} is ambiguous if both (h,m) and (m,h) satisfy the valid-reading constraint and h != m.
  - Only configurations with |h-m| <= 4 are considered.
  - Choose uniformly among all such configurations; the error is |h-m|.
  ask:
  - Compute the expected error E. Write E=a/b in lowest terms with a,b positive integers and output a+b.
answer_spec:
  type: integer
  normalization: exact_integer (a+b for reduced a/b)
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: cycle_length
    type: int
    domain: 12
    purpose: hour-cycle length for the clock model
  - name: accuracy_window
    type: real
    domain: positive
    purpose: bound on |h-m| (here 4)
  - name: selection_rule
    type: other
    domain: uniform_over_finite_set
    purpose: choose uniformly among admissible ambiguous configurations
  invariant(s):
  - name: valid_time_pair_constraint
    depends_on:
    - h
    - m
    statement: Valid readings satisfy h = floor(h) + m/12 (equivalently m = 12*{h}).
  - name: ambiguity_via_swap
    depends_on:
    - h
    - m
    statement: Ambiguity occurs when both (h,m) and (m,h) are valid readings, giving a coupled fractional-part system.
  - name: discrete_error_offsets
    depends_on:
    - cycle_length
    statement: Writing i=floor(h), j=floor(m), solutions satisfy h-m = 12(i-j)/13, so errors are discrete multiples of 12/13.
  - name: window_bounds_integer_parameter
    depends_on:
    - accuracy_window
    statement: The constraint |h-m|<=4 becomes |i-j|<=4. Excluding h=m removes |i-j|=0.
  - name: multiplicity_by_diagonal_count
    depends_on:
    - cycle_length
    statement: For fixed n=|i-j|, the number of unordered configurations is 12-n (count of integer pairs with i>j and i-j=n).
  operator(s):
  - fractional_part
  - integer_part_decomposition
  - solve_linear_system
  - derive_error_formula
  - bound_integer_parameter_by_window
  - count_integer_pairs_on_diagonal
  - expected_value
  - reduce_fraction
  - sum_numer_denom
  essential_assumption(s):
  - Times are modeled on [0,12) with ordinary absolute difference |h-m| (no circular wrap-around).
  - Ambiguity excludes the trivial case h=m.
  - The admissible ambiguous configurations form a finite set, enabling a uniform choice and exact expectation.
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - The fractional-part system reduces to integer-part parameters (i,j) and forces the error |h-m|=12|i-j|/13. The window bounds |i-j| and yields finitely many cases with uniquely determined multiplicities. Therefore the expected error is a unique rational number; its reduced form (a,b) is unique, hence a+b is unique.
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: set_up_fractional_part_equations
    inputs:
    - valid_time_pair_constraint
    - ambiguity_via_swap
    purpose: translate "both readings valid" into equations involving floors (integer parts)
    outputs:
    - linear_system_in_h_m_with_i_j
  - name: derive_discrete_offsets
    inputs:
    - linear_system_in_h_m_with_i_j
    purpose: eliminate h,m to obtain the discrete error formula |h-m|=12|i-j|/13
    outputs:
    - error_offset_formula
  - name: apply_window_and_count_multiplicities
    inputs:
    - accuracy_window
    - error_offset_formula
    purpose: bound |i-j| and count how many configurations realize each offset n via diagonal counting
    outputs:
    - counts_per_n
    - total_count
  - name: compute_expected_error_and_finalize
    inputs:
    - counts_per_n
    purpose: compute E as a weighted average, reduce to a/b, and output a+b
    outputs:
    - out
  theorem(s):
  - name: decomposition_by_integer_parts
    role: floor decomposition turns the fractional-part constraints into linear equations on integer-part regions
  - name: integer_bound_from_inequality
    role: bounding |12n/13|<=4 yields n<=4
  - name: uniqueness_of_reduced_fraction
    role: gcd normalization makes a/b unique
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - reduce a continuous-looking ambiguity to discrete integer parameters and then compute an exact expected value by counting
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Convert the swap-ambiguity constraint into a linear system indexed by integer parts i=floor(h), j=floor(m).
  - Derive the discrete error law |h-m|=12|i-j|/13 and use the window to restrict |i-j|.
  - Count configurations for each offset by counting lattice points on diagonals, then compute the uniform expected error.
  finish_type: discrete_parameter_counting_plus_expectation
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: fractions
    reason: exact rational arithmetic for the expected value and reduction
  import_hint(s):
  - from fractions import Fraction
  task(s):
  - name: enumerate_integer_part_pairs
    reason: loop over i,j in 0..11, construct (h,m), filter |h-m|<=4 and h!=m, and compute the same expectation as a check
  - name: reduce_and_sum
    reason: reduce the expected value Fraction and output numerator+denominator
well_posed: true
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    cycle_length: 12
    accuracy_window: 4
  final_answer:
    value: 787
    normalized: 787

  - 'Agent 1:'

  - (4) not G1
  - 'Agent 2:'
  - (1) G2
  - (2) P2
  - (3) L3
  - (4) (P4 -> not L1)
  - 'Agent 3:'
  - (1) L2
  - (2) not P4
  - (3) (P2 <-> L4)
  - (4) (G2 -> L4)
  - 'Agent 4:'
  - (1) not G4
  - (2) P4
  - (3) (L1 <-> G3)
  - (4) (L1 -> G2)
  ask:
  - Determine the guilty agent. Return a single digit from 1 to 4.

  normalization: none

    type: other
    domain: list of four positive integers
    purpose: records the number of statements made by each agent
  - name: liar_culprit_coupling
    type: enum
    domain:
      distinct: true
      unconstrained: false
    purpose: records whether the liar and culprit must be different agents
  invariant(s):
  - name: unique_liar
    depends_on:
    - L
    statement: Exactly one agent is the universal liar, and every statement made by that agent is false.
  - name: unique_culprit
    depends_on:
    - G
    statement: Exactly one agent is guilty.
  operator(s):
  - case_split_on_liar_identity
  - propositional_constraint_propagation
  - implication
  - biconditional
  - truth_value_fixed_point
  essential_assumption(s):
  - Each agent is globally truthful or globally dishonest across all of their statements.
  - The liar and the guilty agent are distinct.
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - Exhaustive world search leaves a unique guilty label after enforcing all statement truths and any referenced statement-truth variables.
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: exhaustive_world_search
    inputs:
    - liar identity
    - guilty identity
    - presence variables
    - referenced truth variables
    purpose: test all candidate worlds against the statement set and keep only consistent assignments
    outputs:
    - surviving worlds
    - unique culprit label
  theorem(s):
  - name: classical_propositional_semantics
    role: determines the truth of each statement under a candidate world
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - A finite symbolic world model can be checked exactly by exhaustive enumeration.
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Represent the puzzle as a finite constraint system and search over all liar/guilty assignments until only one culprit label survives.
  finish_type: finite_enumeration
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: itertools
    reason: enumerate liar, guilty, presence, and referenced truth-value assignments exactly
  import_hint(s):
  - from itertools import product
  task(s):
  - name: symbolic_world_search
    reason: verify the culprit by exhaustive propositional evaluation
well_posed: 'yes'

  parameter_value(s):
    statement_count_per_agent:
    - 4
    - 4
    - 4
    - 4
    distinct_liar_and_culprit: true
  final_answer:
    value: '2'
    normalized: '2'

problem_spec:
  summary: On a finite grid, a walker makes unit taxicab steps with a no-backtrack constraint. An adversary walks as well, and at each step n the pair {b_n,b_{n+1}} is forbidden for both endpoints of the walker's step n (including n=0). Determine the minimal time t for which the adversary can preselect a path b_1,...,b_t that makes any length-t walker path impossible.
  object(s):

    role: given
    dependency: independent

    type: function
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: a
