source_short_name: Rank and kernel of the 5x5 (i-j)^3 skew-symmetric Toeplitz matrix
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1
schema_version: 1
identity:
  family_id: linear_algebra__signed_distance_power_toeplitz__skew_symmetric__rank_and_kernel_by_pfaffian_and_finite_differences
  instance_id: n5__p3_signed__rank4_kernel_binomial
  short_name: Rank and kernel of the 5x5 (i-j)^3 skew-symmetric Toeplitz matrix
  generation: 1
taxonomy:
  domain: linear_algebra
  subdomain(s):
  - matrix_rank
  - structured_matrices (Toeplitz)
  - skew_symmetric_matrices
  - finite_differences_and_polynomial_sequences
  topic_path:
  - linear_algebra
  - matrices
  - rank and nullspace of structured matrices
  tags:
  - rank
  - nullspace
  - skew_symmetric
  - toeplitz
  - signed_difference
  - polynomial_entries
  - pfaffian
  - finite_difference
problem_spec:
  summary: 'Determine the rank and a nullspace generator for a fixed finite skew-symmetric Toeplitz matrix whose entries are an odd power of the signed index difference (i-j)^p. Use skew-symmetry to bound rank, a nonzero 4x4 minor to certify rank, and a finite-difference identity to construct a kernel vector.

    '
  object(s):
  - name: A
    type: matrix
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: rank(A)
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  - name: v
    type: matrix
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - A is a 5x5 matrix with entries A_{ij} = (i-j)^3 for i,j in {1,2,3,4,5}.
  - Computations are over a field of characteristic 0 (e.g., ℚ or ℝ).
  ask:
  - Compute rank(A) and exhibit a nonzero vector v with Av=0.
answer_spec:
  type: object
  normalization: none
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: n
    type: int
    domain: integer n >= 1
    purpose: matrix dimension (here n=5)
  - name: p
    type: int
    domain: odd_integer p >= 1
    purpose: odd exponent in signed distance power (i-j)^p (here p=3)
  - name: distance_type
    type: enum
    domain:
      signed: null
    purpose: uses (i-j) rather than |i-j|, forcing skew-symmetry when p is odd
  - name: base_field_characteristic
    type: int
    domain: characteristic != 2 (instance uses characteristic 0)
    purpose: ensures skew-symmetric rank parity and Pfaffian determinant identities hold
  invariant(s):
  - name: toeplitz_structure
    depends_on:
    - A
    statement: A_{ij} depends only on (i-j), so A is Toeplitz.
  - name: skew_symmetry_for_odd_p
    depends_on:
    - A
    - p
    statement: For odd p, (i-j)^p = -(j-i)^p, hence A^T = -A.
  - name: even_rank_constraint
    depends_on:
    - A
    - base_field_characteristic
    statement: Over characteristic \neq2, any skew-symmetric matrix has even rank; in odd dimension its determinant is 0.
  - name: polynomial_sequence_rows
    depends_on:
    - A
    statement: For fixed i, the row entries A_{i,j}=(i-j)^3 form a degree-3 polynomial sequence in j.
  - name: finite_difference_annihilator_vector
    depends_on:
    - A
    statement: 'The 4th forward difference of any degree \leq3 polynomial is 0, yielding a kernel vector v=(1,-4,6,-4,1)^T satisfying Av=0 for n=5.

      '
  operator(s):
  - integer_power
  - gaussian_elimination
  - elementary_row_operations
  - pivot_count
  - determinant_minor_test
  - pfaffian
  - finite_difference_operator
  essential_assumption(s):
  - Indices i,j range over consecutive integers 1..n.
  - Rank and nullspace are computed over a field with characteristic not equal to 2 (here characteristic 0).
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - statement: 'Skew-symmetry in odd dimension forces rank(A) \leq n-1, and a nonzero (n-1)x(n-1) minor certifies rank(A) \geq n-1. Together these force rank(A)=n-1 uniquely (here rank=4).

      '
  - statement: 'Once rank(A)=4 for a 5x5 matrix, nullity(A)=1 by rank-nullity, so any nonzero kernel vector spans ker(A), giving a unique kernel line.

      '
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: explicit_matrix_expansion
    inputs:
    - entry rule A_{ij}=(i-j)^3
    purpose: write A as an explicit integer matrix.
    outputs:
    - concrete 5x5 matrix
  - name: skew_symmetry_rank_parity_bound
    inputs:
    - skew-symmetric structure A^T=-A
    purpose: constrain possible ranks (even rank; odd-size determinant 0).
    outputs:
    - rank(A) \leq 4
  - name: nonzero_minor_certificate_via_pfaffian
    inputs:
    - a 4x4 skew-symmetric minor of A
    purpose: certify rank(A) \geq 4 by showing det(minor) \neq 0 using Pfaffian^2 formula.
    outputs:
    - rank(A) \geq 4
  - name: conclude_rank_from_bounds
    inputs:
    - upper and lower rank bounds
    purpose: pin rank exactly.
    outputs:
    - rank(A)=4
  - name: finite_difference_kernel_construction
    inputs:
    - polynomial-in-j row structure
    purpose: build a null vector using vanishing 4th finite differences for cubics.
    outputs:
    - explicit v with Av=0
  - name: rank_nullity_finalize_kernel
    inputs:
    - rank(A) and a nonzero kernel vector
    purpose: deduce ker(A) is 1D and identify its span.
    outputs:
    - ker(A)=span{v}
  theorem(s):
  - name: Elementary row operations preserve rank
    role: Justifies rank certification via minors or row-reduction.
  - name: Skew-symmetric rank parity and odd-size determinant
    role: Over char \neq2, skew-symmetric matrices have even rank; odd dimension implies determinant 0.
  - name: Pfaffian determinant identity (4x4 case)
    role: For 4x4 skew-symmetric matrices, det = (pfaffian)^2.
  - name: Finite difference vanishing
    role: The k-th forward difference of a polynomial of degree <k is identically 0.
  - name: Rank-Nullity Theorem
    role: nullity = n - rank, so a nonzero null vector spans when nullity=1.
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - Linear independence and rank via certified minors.
  - Polynomial sequences annihilated by finite differences.
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Use skew-symmetry (odd size) to restrict rank to \leq4.
  - Prove rank\geq4 by a nonzero 4×4 principal minor, computed cleanly via a Pfaffian.
  - Construct a kernel vector using the vanishing 4th finite difference for cubic sequences.
  finish_type: closed_form
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: sympy
    reason: exact rank, determinant of minors, and nullspace computation over ℚ for verification.
  - name: numpy
    reason: optional floating-point cross-check of rank via SVD (not required for exactness).
  import_hint(s):
  - import sympy as sp
  - import numpy as np
  task(s):
  - name: build_matrix_from_formula
    reason: generate A_{ij}=(i-j)^3 for i,j=1..5.
  - name: exact_rank_and_nullspace
    reason: compute rank(A) and a basis for ker(A) exactly.
  - name: minor_determinant_check
    reason: verify det of the 4x4 principal minor is nonzero.
well_posed: true
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    n: 5
    p: 3
    distance_type: signed
    base_field_characteristic: 0
  final_answer:
    value:
      rank: 4
      kernel_generator:
      - 1
      - -4
      - 6
      - -4
      - 1
    normalized:
      rank: 4
      kernel_generator:
      - 1
      - -4
      - 6
      - -4
      - 1

source_short_name: dim(ker(ad_{J^2}^8) ∩ im(ad_{J^2})) on 20x20 matrices
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1
schema_version: 1
identity:
  family_id: ad_nilpotent_commutator__power_of_shift_splits_blocks__direct_sum_reduction__ker_power_intersect_image_dimension
  instance_id: gl20C_commutator_J2__k8_dim_2026_02_13
  short_name: dim(ker(ad_{J^2}^8) ∩ im(ad_{J^2})) on 20x20 matrices
  generation: 1
taxonomy:
  domain: linear_algebra
  subdomain(s):
  - linear operators on matrix spaces
  - commutator maps and adjoint action
  - nilpotent operators and Jordan-block structure
  - block decompositions and direct sums
  topic_path:
  - linear_algebra
  - linear_operators
  - nilpotent_maps
  - commutator_operator
  tags:
  - commutator_map
  - ad_J
  - power_of_Jordan_block
  - block_diagonal_similarity
  - direct_sum_decomposition
  - ker_power_intersect_image
  - Jordan_block_sizes
problem_spec:
  summary: Let J be the n×n Jordan shift, take N=J^d, and consider the commutator operator T=ad_N on V=M_n(C). Compute dim(ker(T^k) ∩ im(T)) by (i) finding the Jordan type of N=J^d, (ii) reducing T via block decomposition to a direct sum of smaller commutator maps, and (iii) summing the blockwise contributions min(k,b-1).
  object(s):
  - name: n
    type: integer
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: V
    type: other
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: J
    type: matrix
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: d
    type: integer
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: N
    type: matrix
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: T
    type: other
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: k
    type: integer
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: S
    type: other
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: dimS
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - V = M_20(C).
  - J is the 20×20 Jordan-shift matrix with J_{i,i+1}=1 and other entries 0.
  - d = 2 and N = J^d = J^2.
  - T(M)=NM-MN (commutator operator ad_N).
  - k = 8.
  - S = ker(T^k) ∩ im(T).
  ask:
  - Compute dimS = dim_C(S).
answer_spec:
  type: integer
  normalization: none
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: n
    type: int
    domain: integer >= 2
    purpose: ambient matrix size (here 20).
  - name: d
    type: int
    domain: integer >= 1 with d < n
    purpose: power of the Jordan shift used to define N=J^d (here d=2), changing the Jordan type of N.
  - name: k
    type: int
    domain: integer >= 1
    purpose: kernel power in ker(T^k) (here 8).
  invariant(s):
  - name: Jordan_type_of_J_power
    depends_on:
    - n
    - d
    - N
    statement: For J the size-n Jordan shift, N=J^d splits C^n into d invariant chains indexed by residues mod d, giving Jordan blocks whose sizes differ by at most 1. For (n,d)=(20,2), N has Jordan type (10,10), so N is similar to diag(J10,J10).
  - name: blockwise_commutator_direct_sum
    depends_on:
    - N
    - T
    statement: 'If N=diag(N1,N2) is block diagonal, then ad_N on M_{n1+n2} acts blockwise: ad_N([[A,B],[C,D]]) = [[ad_{N1}(A), N1B-BN2],[N2C-CN1, ad_{N2}(D)]]. When N1=N2, all four blocks are governed by the same commutator operator, so ad_N is a direct sum of four copies.'
  - name: Jordan_blocks_of_ad_regular_nilpotent_size_m
    depends_on:
    - m
    statement: For a single Jordan block J_m in M_m(C), ad_{J_m} on M_m(C) is nilpotent with Jordan-block sizes 1,3,5,...,2m-1 (each once).
  - name: intersection_dim_on_single_Jordan_block
    depends_on:
    - k
    - b
    statement: For a nilpotent Jordan block of size b, dim(ker(N^k) ∩ im(N)) = min(k, b-1).
  operator(s):
  - commutator_operator
  - matrix_power
  - similarity_transform
  - block_decomposition
  - direct_sum_decomposition
  - Jordan_decomposition
  - kernel_of_power
  - image_of_operator
  - blockwise_dimension_sum
  essential_assumption(s):
  - Work over an algebraically closed field of characteristic 0 (C) so Jordan decomposition and dimension arguments apply cleanly.
  - J is the standard Jordan shift (one Jordan block) so its powers have the described chain decomposition.
  - Dimensions are computed in the full matrix space M_n(C), and all decompositions are linear direct sums.
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - statement: The Jordan type of N=J^d is uniquely determined by (n,d); for (20,2) it is (10,10). This forces a block-diagonal similarity N ~ diag(J10,J10) and hence a canonical decomposition ad_N ≅ ad_{J10}^{⊕4}. The Jordan-block multiset of ad_{J10} is uniquely {1,3,...,19}, and the contribution of each block to dim(ker(T^k)∩im(T)) is uniquely min(k,b-1). Therefore dimS is a uniquely determined integer.
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: parity_chain_similarity_for_J_squared
    inputs:
    - J (Jordan shift), d=2
    purpose: compute the Jordan type of N=J^2 and exhibit an explicit basis giving N ~ diag(J10,J10).
    outputs:
    - block diagonal form of N with two size-10 Jordan blocks
  - name: block_matrix_decomposition_of_commutator
    inputs:
    - N in block diagonal form and block decomposition of M_20
    purpose: reduce ad_N to a direct sum of smaller commutator maps on 10×10 blocks.
    outputs:
    - operator decomposition ad_N ≅ ad_{J10}^{⊕4}
  - name: invoke_ad_regular_nilpotent_Jordan_spectrum
    inputs:
    - ad_{J10} on M_10(C)
    purpose: identify Jordan-block sizes 1,3,...,19 for the smaller commutator operator.
    outputs:
    - block size multiset for ad_{J10}
  - name: compute_intersection_dim_blockwise
    inputs:
    - block sizes and k
    purpose: compute dim(ker(T0^k)∩im(T0)) as Σ min(k,b-1) over blocks.
    outputs:
    - dim per copy (here 60)
  - name: scale_by_direct_sum_multiplicity
    inputs:
    - 4-fold direct sum decomposition
    purpose: multiply the per-copy dimension by 4 to obtain dimS.
    outputs:
    - dimS (here 240)
  theorem(s):
  - name: Similarity preserves kernel/image dimensions
    role: permits replacing N by a similar block diagonal form.
  - name: Jordan-block description of powers of a Jordan shift
    role: justifies N=J^2 has Jordan type (10,10) via invariant parity chains.
  - name: Jordan block kernel-image fact
    role: on a size-b nilpotent Jordan block, dim(ker(N^k) ∩ im(N)) = min(k,b-1).
  - name: Regular nilpotent adjoint Jordan spectrum
    role: ad_{J_m} on M_m(C) has Jordan blocks 1,3,5,...,2m-1.
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - Decompose the space into invariant direct sums so kernels/images and dimensions add blockwise.
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Replace N=J^2 by a similar block diagonal matrix diag(J10,J10) using the parity-chain basis.
  - Observe that commutation with a block diagonal matrix acts blockwise, giving ad_N as a 4-fold direct sum.
  - Reduce the dimension computation to the known regular-nilpotent ad spectrum in size 10 and a blockwise min-sum.
  finish_type: closed_form
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: sympy
    reason: exact linear algebra for building ad_N as a 400×400 matrix and computing ranks/nullities for verification.
  - name: numpy
    reason: optional fast numeric/sparse checks (not required for exactness).
  import_hint(s):
  - import sympy as sp
  - import numpy as np
  task(s):
  - name: build_operator_matrix_adN
    reason: represent T(M)=NM-MN on a vectorized basis of M_20 and form the 400×400 matrix of T.
  - name: verify_dimension_by_rank_nullity
    reason: compute dim(ker(T^8)∩im(T)) directly (e.g., via bases for ker(T^8) and im(T) and intersection rank).
well_posed: true
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    n: 20
    d: 2
    k: 8
  final_answer:
    value: 240
    normalized: 240

source_short_name: Blow-up limit L*=1; rational-phase trig selector; weighted sum mod 1000
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1
schema_version: 1
identity:
  family_id: singular_riccati_ode__universal_blowup_limit__rational_phase_trig_selector__block_sum_mod1000
  instance_id: xyp_eqn__Lstar1__N2025__pi_phases__Rmod1000_116
  short_name: Blow-up limit L*=1; rational-phase trig selector; weighted sum mod 1000
  generation: 1
taxonomy:
  domain: mixed
  subdomain(s):
  - singular first-order ODE (Riccati-type) and analytic germs
  - finite-time blow-up and normalized asymptotics
  - periodic trigonometric selectors reducible to congruences
  - CRT-style residue intersection and block summation
  topic_path:
  - analysis
  - differential_equations
  - blow_up_asymptotics_and_discrete_postprocessing
  tags:
  - riccati
  - analytic_germ_uniqueness
  - finite_time_blow_up
  - normalized_blowup_limit
  - rational_multiple_of_pi
  - trig_sign_congruence
  - CRT
  - block_sum
  - mod_1000
problem_spec:
  summary: A singular Riccati ODE with a specified real-analytic germ at 0 blows up in finite positive time. A normalized blow-up limit L* is extracted and inserted into trigonometric inequalities whose phases are rational multiples of \pi, converting the selector to congruence conditions. The resulting weighted sum up to N is computed by one-period residue enumeration and block summation, then reduced mod 1000.
  object(s):
  - name: f
    type: function
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: beta
    type: real
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: L_star
    type: real
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: R
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - 'ODE: x f''(x) = x + f(x)^2.'
  - f is real-analytic near 0 with f(0)=0 and f'(0)=1.
  - Let beta be the right endpoint of maximal existence on (0,beta) (beta is finite).
  - Define L_star = lim_{x->beta^-} ((beta-x)/beta) f(x).
  - Define R = sum_{n=1}^{2025} n * 1_{ { cos(pi*n/7 + pi*L_star) > 0 and sin(pi*n/9 - (pi/2)*L_star) < 0 } }.
  - Output requested is R mod 1000.
  ask:
  - Compute R modulo 1000.
answer_spec:
  type: integer_mod_m
  normalization: mod_m
  modulus: 1000
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: N
    type: int
    domain: integer_N_ge_1
    purpose: upper limit of the weighted sum (here 2025)
  - name: trig_denominators
    type: other
    domain: (7,9)
    purpose: sets periods 14 and 18, hence joint period 126
  - name: phase_recipe
    type: other
    domain: phases_are_rational_multiples_of_pi_in_L_star
    purpose: ensures trig sign tests reduce to congruences once L_star is known
  - name: output_modulus
    type: int
    domain: m=1000
    purpose: reduce final integer modulo 1000
  invariant(s):
  - name: analytic_uniqueness_via_series_recursion
    depends_on:
    - f
    statement: Writing f(x)=sum a_n x^n with a_0=0,a_1=1 and matching coefficients in x f'=x+f^2 yields a unique recursion for a_n (n>=2), hence a unique analytic germ at 0.
  - name: finite_positive_blow_up_time
    depends_on:
    - beta
    statement: For x>0, f'(x)=1+f(x)^2/x>0 and comparison f' >= f^2/x forces a finite blow-up time beta<infty.
  - name: normalized_blowup_limit_universal
    depends_on:
    - beta
    - L_star
    statement: As x->beta^-, f(x)->+infty and f'/f^2 = 1/x + 1/f^2 -> 1/beta, implying 1/f(x) ~ (beta-x)/beta and therefore L_star = 1.
  - name: congruence_reduction_of_trig_signs
    depends_on:
    - L_star
    - trig_denominators
    - phase_recipe
    statement: With phases pi*L_star and (pi/2)*L_star, and with L_star=1, the sign conditions become cos(pi*n/7)<0 and cos(pi*n/9)>0, depending only on n mod 14 and mod 18 respectively.
  - name: periodic_selector_joint_period
    depends_on:
    - trig_denominators
    statement: The joint selector is periodic in n with period lcm(14,18)=126, enabling block summation up to N.
  operator(s):
  - power_series_coefficient_matching
  - differential_inequality_integration
  - maximal_interval_extension
  - asymptotic_limit_extraction
  - trigonometric_period_reduction
  - congruence_classification
  - chinese_remainder_theorem
  - arithmetic_progression_block_sum
  - modular_reduction
  essential_assumption(s):
  - The analytic solution through x=0 is selected by f(0)=0,f'(0)=1.
  - Continuation is valid for x>0 as long as f stays finite.
  - Strict trig inequalities are used; the chosen phases avoid zero cases for integer n.
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - The series recursion determines f uniquely near 0; maximal continuation determines beta uniquely. The normalization identity forces L_star=1 uniquely. Substituting L_star fixes the congruence-based selector uniquely on a finite period, so the resulting weighted sum and its residue mod 1000 are unique.
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: series_solution_and_recursion
    inputs:
    - ODE
    - initial_data
    purpose: certify local analytic existence/uniqueness and fix the germ at 0
    outputs:
    - unique_germ
  - name: finite_time_blow_up_via_comparison
    inputs:
    - f' >= f^2/x
    purpose: prove beta is finite
    outputs:
    - beta_finite
  - name: normalized_blowup_asymptotic
    inputs:
    - limit_of_f_over_beta_minus_x
    purpose: compute L_star without solving for beta explicitly
    outputs:
    - L_star_equals_1
  - name: trig_signs_to_congruences
    inputs:
    - L_star_equals_1
    - phase_recipe
    purpose: convert trig inequalities into residue classes mod 14 and 18
    outputs:
    - residue_conditions
  - name: CRT_and_one_period_enumeration
    inputs:
    - residue_conditions
    purpose: find the valid residues mod 126 and compute their count and sum
    outputs:
    - S_residues
    - count_S
    - sum_S
  - name: block_sum_and_mod
    inputs:
    - N
    - S_residues
    - count_S
    - sum_S
    purpose: compute the weighted sum up to N by full periods plus remainder, then reduce mod 1000
    outputs:
    - R_mod_1000
  theorem(s):
  - name: analytic_existence_uniqueness_for_ODE
    role: supports uniqueness of the analytic germ fixed by recursion
  - name: ODE_continuation_theorem
    role: rules out finite beta with finite f(beta^-)
  - name: asymptotic_from_ratio_limit
    role: if f'/f^2 -> 1/beta and f->infty then (beta-x)f/beta -> 1
  - name: trigonometric_periodicity
    role: cos(pi*(n+14)/7)=cos(pi*n/7), cos(pi*(n+18)/9)=cos(pi*n/9)
  - name: chinese_remainder_theorem_with_gcd_2
    role: solvability and uniqueness mod 126 for congruences mod 14 and 18
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - comparison inequality for blow-up
  - periodic reduction to finite residue enumeration
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Extract L_star=1 from universal blow-up normalization.
  - Choose phases as rational multiples of \pi so trig sign tests become pure congruences.
  - Use one-period enumeration plus block summation to compute the weighted sum and reduce mod 1000.
  finish_type: counting_argument
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: mpmath
    reason: optional numerical verification of L_star=1 and trig-sign checks
  - name: itertools
    reason: optional enumeration of residues mod 126 and block-sum verification
  import_hint(s):
  - import mpmath as mp
  - from itertools import product
  task(s):
  - name: verify_residue_set
    reason: enumerate n=1..126 and confirm the 32 valid residues
  - name: verify_block_sum
    reason: compute R up to N=2025 directly and confirm the closed-form block computation
  - name: mod_1000_reduce
    reason: reduce the final integer to its residue mod 1000
well_posed: true
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    N: 2025
    trig_denominators:
    - 7
    - 9
    phases:
      cos_shift: pi*L_star
      sin_shift: -(pi/2)*L_star
    output_modulus: 1000
  final_answer:
    value: 116
    normalized: 116

source_short_name: Count integer (a,b) with P(1)=0 and exactly one root in (0,1)
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1
schema_version: 1
identity:
  family_id: parameterized_polynomial__convexity_transform__anchored_root_at_one__unit_interval_single_root_count
  instance_id: quintic_ax3_bx2_plus1_root_at_1_exactly_one_root_in_0_1_ab_2026_02_13
  short_name: Count integer (a,b) with P(1)=0 and exactly one root in (0,1)
  generation: 1
taxonomy:
  domain: mixed
  subdomain(s):
  - polynomial real-root counting with parameter constraints
  - convexity/derivative methods for root location
  - lattice-point counting on affine constraints
  topic_path:
  - algebra
  - real polynomials
  - parameter counting via convexity and anchored roots
  tags:
  - real_root
  - convexity
  - intermediate_value
  - derivative_sign
  - anchored_root
  - parameter_count
  - lattice_points
problem_spec:
  summary: With integer parameters (a,b) in a box and constrained by P(1)=0, determine when a quintic has exactly one real root in (0,1) using a convex auxiliary function and a derivative-sign criterion, then count all lattice points satisfying the conditions.
  object(s):
  - name: a
    type: integer
    role: unknown
    dependency: independent
  - name: b
    type: integer
    role: unknown
    dependency: independent
  - name: P
    type: polynomial
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: H
    type: function
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: count
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - Integers a,b satisfy |a| \leq 6 and |b| \leq 6.
  - P(x)=x^5 + a x^3 + b x^2 + 1.
  - 'Anchoring constraint: P(1)=0 (equivalently a+b=-2).'
  - The target interval is (0,1).
  ask:
  - Count ordered pairs (a,b) satisfying the givens such that P(x)=0 has exactly one real root in (0,1).
answer_spec:
  type: count
  normalization: none
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: a
    type: int
    domain: integers_in_range
    purpose: controls the cubic-term contribution and (via H'(1)=a+1) the location of the convex minimum relative to x=1
  - name: b
    type: int
    domain: integers_in_range
    purpose: controls the vertical shift of the auxiliary convex function H
  - name: anchored_root_point
    type: other
    domain:
      '1': null
    purpose: imposes the affine constraint P(1)=0, i.e. forces the line b=-a-2 in the (a,b)-lattice
  - name: interval
    type: other
    domain: (0,1)
    purpose: interval in which the unique root is required
  - name: transform_choice
    type: other
    domain: divide_by_x^2_and_analyze_convex_helper
    purpose: converts root counting for P on (0,∞) to convex analysis for H
  invariant(s):
  - name: root_equivalence
    depends_on:
    - P
    - H
    statement: For x>0, P(x)=0 iff H(x)=P(x)/x^2=0.
  - name: strict_convexity
    depends_on:
    - a
    statement: H''(x)=6x+6/x^4>0 for x>0, so H is strictly convex on (0,∞).
  - name: anchored_value_at_one
    depends_on:
    - a
    - b
    statement: P(1)=a+b+2; the constraint P(1)=0 enforces H(1)=0.
  - name: derivative_at_one
    depends_on:
    - a
    statement: H'(1)=a+1, so the sign of a+1 determines whether the minimizer of H lies left or right of 1.
  - name: derivative_sign_criterion_for_root_left_of_one
    depends_on:
    - H
    statement: Under H(1)=0 and strict convexity, H has a (necessarily unique) root in (0,1) iff H'(1)>0.
  operator(s):
  - polynomial_to_auxiliary_function (divide by x^2)
  - derivative_and_convexity_test
  - intermediate_value_theorem
  - inequality_filter_on_parameters
  - lattice_point_count
  essential_assumption(s):
  - Consider x>0 when dividing by x^2.
  - Real analysis facts (strict convexity, monotonicity of H', IVT) apply on (0,∞).
  - The constraint P(1)=0 is enforced exactly in integers (a,b).
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - The condition P(1)=0 forces b=-a-2, reducing the parameter search to a finite set of integers a.
  - 'Strict convexity of H gives a rigid equivalence: existence of a root in (0,1) is determined solely by the sign of H''(1)=a+1.'
  - Therefore the set of admissible (a,b) is uniquely determined by finitely many inequalities and has a unique count.
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: convex_helper_function
    inputs:
    - P(x) and restriction x>0
    purpose: produce a strictly convex function whose zeros correspond to positive zeros of P
    outputs:
    - H(x)=x^3+ax+b+1/x^2 with H''(x)>0
  - name: anchored_root_reduction
    inputs:
    - constraint P(1)=0
    purpose: reduce the 2D lattice search to an affine line b=-a-2
    outputs:
    - one-parameter family indexed by a
  - name: derivative_sign_classification_at_anchor
    inputs:
    - strict convexity of H and the anchored condition H(1)=0
    purpose: decide whether an additional root lies in (0,1) via the sign of H'(1)
    outputs:
    - criterion H has a root in (0,1) ⇔ H'(1)>0 ⇔ a\geq0 (for integer a)
  - name: lattice_count_on_affine_constraint
    inputs:
    - bounds |a|,|b|\leq6 and b=-a-2 and a\geq0
    purpose: count admissible integer pairs
    outputs:
    - final count
  theorem(s):
  - name: Intermediate Value Theorem
    role: certify existence of a root in (0,1) from a sign change between 0+ and the minimum point
  - name: Strict convexity implies monotone derivative and at most two zeros
    role: guarantees uniqueness of the minimizer and bounds the number of positive roots
  - name: Convex anchored-zero derivative criterion
    role: for strictly convex H with H(1)=0, a root in (0,1) exists iff H'(1)>0
  - name: basic lattice counting on a line segment in a box
    role: count integer points satisfying b=-a-2 with bound constraints and a-inequality
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - Convexity and monotonicity of derivatives control root existence; counting reduces to integer points on an affine constraint.
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Divide by x^2 to obtain a strictly convex auxiliary function H on (0,∞).
  - Use the anchoring constraint P(1)=0 (so H(1)=0) and strict convexity to reduce root existence in (0,1) to the sign of H'(1)=a+1.
  - Count integer a satisfying the resulting inequalities and bounds for b=-a-2.
  finish_type: counting_argument
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: sympy
    reason: optional exact verification of the (a,b) list by isolating real roots in (0,1)
  - name: itertools
    reason: optional brute-force enumeration over the finite lattice box for verification
  import_hint(s):
  - from itertools import product
  - import sympy as sp
  task(s):
  - name: brute_force_verify_count_under_anchor
    reason: iterate all (a,b) in [-6,6]^2 with a+b=-2 and count real roots of P in (0,1) to confirm the analytic criterion
well_posed: true
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    a_range:
    - -6
    - 6
    b_range:
    - -6
    - 6
    anchored_root_point: 1
    interval: (0,1)
  final_answer:
    value: 5
    normalized: 5

source_short_name: Minimize complement-of-cycle edge sum under universal cycle constraint
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1
schema_version: 1
identity:
  family_id: cyclic_shift_quadratic_form__permutation_min_constraint__cycle_complement_objective__hamilton_decomposition_sharp_min
  instance_id: n101_exclude_fixed_cycle__Qge_neg14__min_abs_Phi
  short_name: Minimize complement-of-cycle edge sum under universal cycle constraint
  generation: 2
taxonomy:
  domain: mixed
  subdomain(s):
  - quadratic forms from graph adjacency (cycle graph)
  - permutation-invariant constraints (min over S_n)
  - extremal pair-sum minimization on subgraphs of K_n
  - Hamiltonian cycle decompositions of complete graphs
  topic_path:
  - combinatorics
  - graph_theory
  - hamiltonian_decompositions
  - extremal_inequalities
  - linear_algebra
  - quadratic_forms
  tags:
  - cyclic_shift_matrix
  - adjacency_matrix
  - quadratic_form
  - permutation_invariance
  - Hamilton_decomposition
  - Walecki
  - complement_of_cycle
  - sharp_lower_bound
  - two_level_extremizer
  - n_odd
problem_spec:
  summary: For odd n, impose a universal lower bound on the cyclic adjacent-product sum across all permutations via the quadratic form u^T(M+M^T)u. Minimize a modified complete-graph pair-sum objective that excludes the edges of one fixed Hamilton cycle C (the base cycle), i.e. Phi = Psi - u^T(M+M^T)u. Use a Hamilton decomposition of K_n that includes C to express Phi/2 as a sum of (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycle weights, apply the per-cycle bound, and construct a two-level vector making every Hamilton cycle weight constant to attain the bound.
  object(s):
  - name: n
    type: integer
    role: given
    dependency: independent
  - name: M
    type: matrix
    role: given
    dependency: dependent
  - name: P
    type: matrix
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: v
    type: vector
    role: unknown
    dependency: dependent
  - name: Q_P
    type: function
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: Psi
    type: function
    role: aux
    dependency: dependent
  - name: Phi
    type: function
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  - name: out
    type: integer
    role: output
    dependency: dependent
  givens:
  - 'n is an odd integer (instance: n=101).'
  - M is the n×n cyclic shift matrix, so M+M^T is the adjacency matrix of the cycle C_n.
  - For permutation matrix P, Q_P(v)=v^T P^T(M+M^T)Pv.
  - 'v is admissible if min_P Q_P(v) >= -C (instance: C=14).'
  - Psi(v)=(sum_i v_i)^2 - sum_i v_i^2.
  - Define Phi(v)=Psi(v) - v^T(M+M^T)v (equivalently, twice the total weight on edges of K_n excluding the base cycle edges).
  ask:
  - Compute | min Phi(v) | over all admissible v.
answer_spec:
  type: integer
  normalization: exact_integer
family_structure:
  parameter(s):
  - name: n
    type: int
    domain: odd_integer >= 3
    purpose: dimension; ensures existence of a Hamilton decomposition of K_n
  - name: constraint_level_C
    type: real
    domain: positive
    purpose: lower bound on min_P Q_P(v)
  - name: base_graph
    type: graph
    domain: cycle_Cn
    purpose: M+M^T encodes adjacency on the cycle used in the permutation-min constraint
  - name: distinguished_hamilton_cycle
    type: graph
    domain: a fixed Hamilton cycle C in K_n (typically the base cycle (1,2,...,n,1))
    purpose: determines which K_n edges are excluded in the objective Phi
  invariant(s):
  - name: quadratic_form_as_cycle_edge_sum
    depends_on:
    - M
    statement: For u=Pv, Q_P(v)=u^T(M+M^T)u equals 2*sum_{i mod n} u_i u_{i+1}, i.e., twice the weighted edge sum along the Hamilton cycle order given by u.
  - name: admissibility_is_universal_cycle_bound
    depends_on:
    - v
    - constraint_level_C
    statement: 'min_P Q_P(v) >= -C is equivalent to: for every permutation u of v, sum_{i mod n} u_i u_{i+1} >= -C/2.'
  - name: Psi_is_complete_graph_pair_sum
    depends_on:
    - v
    statement: Psi(v) = (sum v_i)^2 - sum v_i^2 = 2*sum_{1<=i<j<=n} v_i v_j, i.e., twice the total edge weight of K_n with weights v_i v_j.
  - name: Phi_is_complement_of_distinguished_cycle
    depends_on:
    - v
    - M
    statement: v^T(M+M^T)v = 2*sum_{edges of the distinguished cycle C} v_i v_j. Therefore Phi(v)=Psi(v)-v^T(M+M^T)v equals 2*(total edge weight on K_n \ C).
  - name: hamilton_decomposition_including_fixed_cycle
    depends_on:
    - n
    - distinguished_hamilton_cycle
    statement: For odd n, K_n decomposes into (n-1)/2 edge-disjoint Hamilton cycles. By relabeling vertices, we may assume the decomposition contains the distinguished Hamilton cycle C. Hence K_n \ C decomposes into (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycles.
  operator(s):
  - permutation_action_on_vectors
  - adjacency_quadratic_form_evaluation
  - rewrite_Psi_as_pair_sum
  - subtract_distinguished_cycle_edges
  - Hamiltonian_cycle_decomposition
  - sum_bounds_over_cycles
  - construct_extremizer_two_level_vector
  essential_assumption(s):
  - n is odd so a Hamilton decomposition of K_n exists (Walecki).
  - The admissibility constraint uses min over all permutation matrices P (equivalently, all Hamilton cycle orderings of the multiset of entries).
  - v is allowed to be any real vector (not restricted to integers).
  uniqueness_certificate(s):
  - 'Lower bound: write Phi/2 as the sum of (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycle edge sums via a Hamilton decomposition of K_n that includes the distinguished cycle. Each such Hamilton cycle sum equals sum u_i u_{i+1} for some permutation u of v, hence is >= -C/2 by admissibility. Therefore Phi >= -(n-3)C/2. Attainment: choose a two-level vector with one entry a and the remaining entries b so that every Hamilton cycle sum is the same constant 2ab+(n-2)b^2 = -C/2, forcing equality in all cycle bounds simultaneously. Hence min Phi is uniquely -(n-3)C/2 and |min Phi| is uniquely (n-3)C/2.'
reasoning_components:
  technique(s):
  - name: convert_constraint_to_cycle_sums
    inputs:
    - Q_P(v)=v^T P^T(M+M^T)Pv
    - cyclic shift matrix M
    purpose: translate the permutation-min quadratic constraint into a uniform lower bound on cyclic adjacent products
    outputs:
    - inequality sum u_i u_{i+1} >= -C/2 for all permutations u
  - name: express_objective_as_complete_graph_minus_cycle_weight
    inputs:
    - Psi(v)
    - v^T(M+M^T)v
    purpose: reinterpret Phi as twice the total edge weight of K_n with one Hamilton cycle removed
    outputs:
    - Phi/2 equals total weight on K_n \ C
  - name: apply_hamilton_decomposition_with_distinguished_cycle
    inputs:
    - K_n, n odd
    - chosen Hamilton cycle C
    purpose: decompose K_n as C plus (n-3)/2 other Hamilton cycles so K_n \ C is covered by cycles
    outputs:
    - representation of Phi/2 as a sum over (n-3)/2 Hamilton cycle weights
  - name: aggregate_bounds
    inputs:
    - per-cycle bound >= -C/2
    - number of cycles (n-3)/2
    purpose: obtain the sharp global lower bound on Phi
    outputs:
    - Phi >= -(n-3)C/2
  - name: extremal_vector_construction_two_level
    inputs:
    - n, C
    purpose: build v with one value a and n-1 values b making every Hamilton cycle sum constant and equal to -C/2
    outputs:
    - admissible v attaining Phi = -(n-3)C/2
  - name: conclude_min_and_absolute_value
    inputs:
    - lower bound
    - attained value
    purpose: conclude the unique minimum and output its absolute value
    outputs:
    - out = (n-3)C/2 for the instance
  theorem(s):
  - name: Walecki_hamilton_decomposition
    role: for odd n, K_n decomposes into (n-1)/2 edge-disjoint Hamilton cycles
  - name: relabeling_preserves_decomposition
    role: applying a vertex permutation to a Hamilton decomposition yields another decomposition, allowing inclusion of a chosen Hamilton cycle
  - name: adjacency_matrix_quadratic_form_identity
    role: u^T(M+M^T)u equals twice the cyclic adjacent-product sum along the Hamilton order
  - name: algebraic_identity_for_Psi
    role: Psi(v) = (sum v_i)^2 - sum v_i^2 = 2*sum_{i<j} v_i v_j
  closest_first_principle_concept_applied:
  - transform a permutation-min constraint into a uniform Hamilton-cycle inequality and globalize via Hamilton decompositions
solution_signature:
  critical_trick(s):
  - Interpret the constraint as a bound on every Hamilton cycle edge sum after permutation.
  - Rewrite the objective as the complete-graph edge sum with one Hamilton cycle removed, and decompose the remaining edges into Hamilton cycles.
  - Use a two-level vector with one special entry to make every Hamilton cycle sum constant, achieving equality.
  finish_type: sharp_lower_bound_plus_attainment
computational_context:
  preferred_language: python
  library(s):
  - name: networkx
    reason: optional sanity check by constructing Hamilton decompositions for small odd n
  - name: sympy
    reason: optional symbolic verification of Phi(v) and the quadratic form identities
  import_hint(s):
  - import networkx as nx
  - import sympy as sp
  task(s):
  - name: verify_small_n_instances
    reason: brute-force over permutations for small odd n to confirm the formula min Phi = -(n-3)C/2
  - name: check_two_level_extremizer
    reason: confirm that the constructed (a,b)-vector makes all Hamilton cycle sums equal and satisfies the constraint
  - name: compute_instance_value
    reason: evaluate Phi(v) and the bound for the chosen n and C
well_posed: true
instance_snapshot:
  parameter_value(s):
    n: 101
    constraint_level_C: 14
  final_answer:
    value: 686
    normalized: 686

source_short_name: Random 3x3 ODE + polynomial-sum divisibility (n=7 variant)
text:
Athena Canon v2.1 breakdown record. Read this as problem-decomposition memory: identify the objects, givens, ask, invariants, techniques, critical trick, finish type, and answer-normalization contract.

schema_id: distillator_dsl.math.v2.1

identity:

  short_name: Random 3x3 ODE + polynomial-sum divisibility (n=7 variant)

taxonomy:

The event requires a Wronskian-type determinant det[X,X',X''] to be nonzero for all t\geq0 and a separate polynomial-sum invariant Θ_T to be divisible by 6. Compute P(E) exactly and output a three-digit residue derived from the reduced fraction.
